resume:
Renata Salcedo
Operations Manager | E-commerce fulfillment & last-mile

Northgate Logistics — Operations Manager (Mar 2020 – present)
- Run daily operations for a regional fulfillment center processing 40,000 units/day
- Reduced cost-per-order by 22% through a continuous-improvement program
- Managed a team of 60 associates across two shifts

Northgate Logistics — Operations Manager (2021 – present)
- Led the launch of a new sortation center; owned the P&L end to end

Brightway Retail — Shift Supervisor (2017 – 2020)
- Oversaw inbound receiving and putaway for a 200k sq ft DC

Note: resume lists Operations Manager start as both Mar 2020 and 2021 for the same employer; team size appears as 60 in one bullet and 'led a 25-person team' in the cover note. Education: BBA, Supply Chain.
jd: Northgate Logistics is hiring an Operations Manager to run a regional fulfillment center (35k–45k units/day) and drive a continuous-improvement agenda on cost-per-order, throughput, and on-time dispatch. You'll own the daily operating plan across shifts, manage front-line associates and team leads, and partner with engineering on automation rollouts. We want an operator who can attribute a result to the specific changes they personally drove — labor model, slotting, process redesign — and defend the math. Requirements: 4+ years in fulfillment/last-mile operations, demonstrated cost or throughput wins with real numbers, and people-leadership of 25+ associates. Lean/Six Sigma a plus.

history.q: You list a 22% cost-per-order reduction through a continuous-improvement program. Walk me through exactly what you changed and how you know it drove that number.

candidate_last_answer: Absolutely. So this was a holistic, data-driven continuous-improvement journey grounded in operational excellence and a relentless focus on the customer. We took a first-principles approach, broke down silos, and drove a culture of ownership and accountability across the floor. By empowering the team and leveraging best-in-class lean methodologies, we unlocked significant efficiencies and moved the needle on cost-per-order in a really meaningful way. It was all about doing more with less while never compromising on quality — a true win-win that I'm super proud of. At the end of the day it's about people, process, and technology coming together.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: leveraging best-in-class lean methodologies, we unlocked significant efficiencies

expected-yield: the actual arithmetic behind the 22% — baseline cost-per-order, the post-change number, the single biggest lever and its individual contribution, and how the change was isolated from volume effects, replacing the buzzwords with defensible figures
